input.content:
# racp

A small Rust bridge that puts a remote UI in front of a locally running ACP
agent (Kiro CLI, Claude Agent CLI, Gemini CLI, Codex, or anything else that
speaks the [Agent Client Protocol](https://agentclientprotocol.com) over
stdio).

## What it does, in one paragraph

`racp` is an **ACP client**. When a browser connects, `racp` spawns your
configured agent binary as a child process and speaks JSON-RPC 2.0 with it
over stdio. User messages from the browser become `session/prompt` requests;
`session/update` notifications from the agent stream back to the browser as
terminal-style text. The agent keeps its own credentials and model choice;
`racp` carries none.

## Architecture

```
  browser  <--WS-->  racp (this crate)  <--stdio JSON-RPC-->  agent
                      |                                        (kiro-cli acp,
                      |                                         claude,
                      |                                         gemini,
                      |                                         codex, ...)
                      |
                  transport layer
                  (cloudflared today, telegram TODO)
```

- One browser WebSocket connection = one `racp` session = one freshly spawned
  agent subprocess = one ACP session.
- `racp` listens only on `127.0.0.1`. Public reachability is delegated to an
  existing Cloudflare Tunnel on your network.
- The terminal-style web UI is a single embedded HTML file (`src/ui.html`)
  compiled into the binary via `include_str!`.

## File layout

```
RACP/
├── Cargo.toml
├── src/
│   ├── main.rs     # config, ACP bridge, transports, single binary entry
│   └── ui.html     # embedded terminal-style web UI (served at /)
├── cloudflared/
│   └── config.yml  # example named-tunnel config (only for a fresh tunnel)
└── README.md
```

## Quick start

```sh
cd /Users/stefan/Development/RACP
cargo run --release
```

First launch, no config present: `racp` drops into an interactive setup and
writes `~/.racp/config.toml`. Re-run setup any time:

```sh
cargo run --release -- init
```

Then point a browser at `http://127.0.0.1:7842` to smoke-test locally, or at
your public hostname once the tunnel is wired.

## Configuration reference

`~/.racp/config.toml`:

```toml
# Transport picks how clients reach racp.
#   "cloudflared" — serves HTTP + WebSocket on `bind`, for an external tunnel.
#   "telegram"    — stub; long-polls a Telegram bot. Not yet implemented.
transport = "cloudflared"

# Local bind address. Keep it on loopback; public exposure is the tunnel's job.
bind = "127.0.0.1:7842"

# Command to launch the ACP agent. Either a bare name (resolved via $PATH) or
# an absolute path. For Kiro: `kiro-cli` with args ["acp"].
agent_cmd = "kiro-cli"
agent_args = ["acp"]

[telegram]
# Only used when transport = "telegram". Create with @BotFather.
token = ""
```

### Agent command examples

| Agent | `agent_cmd` | `agent_args` |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| Kiro CLI | `kiro-cli` | `["acp"]` |
| Claude Agent CLI | `claude` | check your install; may be `["acp"]` or similar |
| Gemini CLI | `gemini` | check your install |
| Codex | `codex` | check your install |

Confirm a working invocation by running it manually; a healthy ACP agent prints
nothing to stdout until it receives a JSON-RPC message on stdin.

## Expose via an existing Cloudflare Tunnel

If you already have `cloudflared` running (Proxmox LXC, Docker, systemd unit,
whatever), add one ingress rule to its config:

```yaml
ingress:
  # ... your existing rules above ...
  - hostname: racp.example.com
    service: http://<host-running-racp>:7842
  # keep the catch-all last
  - service: http_status:404
```

Route the hostname to the tunnel once from the machine that owns the tunnel
credentials:

```sh
cloudflared tunnel route dns <your-tunnel-name> racp.example.com
```

Reload `cloudflared`. That is it. WebSocket upgrades are forwarded by default,
so `/ws` needs no special flags.

## Put Cloudflare Access in front (strongly recommended)

Once a public hostname points at `racp`, anyone who finds the URL can drive
your local agent. Treat this as non-optional:

1. Cloudflare Zero Trust → **Access → Applications → Add application → Self-hosted**.
2. Application domain: `racp.example.com`.
3. Policy: allow only your email, passkey, or IdP identity.

Access injects a signed `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header on every request.
Validating it in `racp` is a TODO (see *Known gaps*).

## Wire protocol

### Browser ↔ racp (over the WebSocket at `/ws`)

All frames are JSON text.

**Browser → racp:**

```json
{ "type": "prompt", "text": "hello" }
```

**racp → browser:**

```json
{ "type": "append", "role": "user" | "agent" | "sys", "text": "..." }
{ "type": "prompt_done" }
{ "type": "error", "message": "..." }
```

The browser appends `{role}`-classed text into the scrollback; roles drive
colour. `prompt_done` re-enables the input after a turn completes.

### racp ↔ agent (over stdio, ACP JSON-RPC 2.0)

Methods currently exercised:

- `initialize` — negotiates protocol version and capabilities on spawn.
- `session/new` — creates one session per browser connection. `cwd` is
  `racp`'s current working directory.
- `session/prompt` — forwards each user message.
- `session/request_permission` — handled by auto-allowing the first offered
  option. See *Known gaps*.

Notifications handled (`session/update` with `sessionUpdate` variants):

- `agent_message_chunk` → appended as `agent` text in the UI.
- `agent_thought_chunk` → appended as `sys` text with a `(thinking)` prefix.
  Kiro's ACP agent does not emit these at the time of writing; reasoning-model
  agents may.
- `tool_call` / `tool_call_update` → appended as `sys` lines
  `[title — status]`.

Kiro also emits `_kiro.dev/*` extension notifications (slash-command
availability, MCP init status, compaction progress, etc.). `racp` ignores them
today; wire them up in `handle_agent_message` if you want them surfaced.

## Running the binary directly

```sh
cargo build --release
./target/release/racp           # run
./target/release/racp init      # re-run setup
```

Stderr carries `racp`'s own logs and, prefixed with `[agent]`, the agent's
stderr. For Kiro-side debugging:

```sh
KIRO_LOG_LEVEL=debug ./target/release/racp
```

(the env var is inherited by the spawned `kiro-cli acp` child).

## Known gaps (pick up here)

All marked with `TODO:` in the source.

1. **Permission UI.** `session/request_permission` currently auto-allows the
   first offered option. The browser needs a button row and `racp` needs to
   forward options to it and wait for the response. See
   `handle_agent_message` in `src/main.rs`.
2. **Auth enforcement.** `racp` trusts everything that reaches the WebSocket
   upgrade. When fronted by Cloudflare Access, validate the
   `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header (JWKS at
   `https://<team>.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/certs`). See
   `ws_upgrade` in `src/main.rs`.
3. **Telegram transport.** `run_telegram` is a stub. Planned shape: long-poll
   `getUpdates`, one ACP agent per Telegram chat, stream chunks as
   `editMessageText` throttled to ~1/s, inline keyboard for permission
   prompts. Per-user-token model (BotFather) keeps `racp` out of the data
   path.
4. **Kiro extensions (`_kiro.dev/*`).** Not forwarded. Useful candidates:
   slash-command availability (could become a UI affordance), MCP OAuth URL
   (needs user redirect), compaction status (status-bar hint).
5. **Session reuse.** Each browser reload kills its agent and spawns a fresh
   one. Reusing sessions via `session/load` would keep history across
   reconnects. Kiro persists sessions at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`.
6. **Streamable HTTP remote transport.** ACP's draft RFD defines an HTTP/WS
   remote transport with `Acp-Connection-Id` and `Acp-Session-Id` headers;
   today `racp` is purely a local stdio client. Once the RFD stabilises and
   agents support it, `racp` can become a thin remote adapter too.

## Development guide

### Build, check, lint

```sh
cargo check
cargo build --release
cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings   # team should gate on this
```

Rust edition 2021, MSRV follows the current stable toolchain.

### Where to add things

| Change | File and function |
|--------|-------------------|
| New `session/update` variant | `handle_agent_message` in `src/main.rs` |
| Permission UI | split `session/request_permission` handling; extend browser ↔ racp protocol |
| Auth middleware | wrap `Router` in `run_cloudflared` or apply to the `/ws` route |
| New transport (telegram, matrix, ...) | mirror `run_cloudflared`; add variant to the `Transport` enum |
| UI tweaks | `src/ui.html` — no build step, `cargo run` rebuilds |

### Testing

There are no tests yet. Practical coverage to add:

- **Config round-trip.** Property test that `Config` survives
  `toml::to_string_pretty` → `toml::from_str`.
- **JSON-RPC routing.** Unit test the reader task: feed it a mix of
  responses (by id), notifications, and malformed lines, assert the right
  routing.
- **End-to-end smoke.** Spawn `racp` with `agent_cmd = "bash"` and
  `agent_args = ["-c", "<echo canned JSON-RPC>"]` as a fake agent.

### Debugging

- `cargo run --release` prints bind address on stderr and forwards the
  child's stderr with `[agent]` prefix.
- Run the agent manually first to confirm the invocation works:
  `echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{}}' | kiro-cli acp`
  should respond with JSON.
- Browser devtools → Network → WS view shows every frame in both directions.

## Troubleshooting

**`failed to spawn \`kiro-cli\``**
 `agent_cmd` not on `$PATH`. Use `which kiro-cli` and put the absolute path in
 `~/.racp/config.toml`.

**Browser connects, no response after send**
 Check the `[agent]` lines on `racp`'s stderr. Most often the agent is
 prompting for an auth/setup step that only appears in interactive TTYs.
 Finish setup by running the agent once interactively first.

**Cloudflare hostname returns 502**
 The `cloudflared` machine cannot reach the `racp` machine. Check
 `service: http://<host>:7842` in the ingress rule resolves and the port is
 open on the `racp` host.

**WebSocket closes immediately**
 Cloudflare Access policy is rejecting the upgrade. Hit the hostname in a
 browser first to satisfy Access, then retry.

## Licence and ownership

Internal project. Licence TBD before any external distribution.

# racp

A small Rust bridge that puts a remote UI in front of a locally running ACP
agent (Kiro CLI, Claude Agent CLI, Gemini CLI, Codex, or anything else that
speaks the [Agent Client Protocol](https://agentclientprotocol.com) over
stdio).

## What it does, in one paragraph

`racp` is an **ACP client**. When a browser connects, `racp` spawns your
configured agent binary as a child process and speaks JSON-RPC 2.0 with it
over stdio. User messages from the browser become `session/prompt` requests;
`session/update` notifications from the agent stream back to the browser as
terminal-style text. The agent keeps its own credentials and model choice;
`racp` carries none.

## Architecture

```
  browser  <--WS-->  racp (this crate)  <--stdio JSON-RPC-->  agent
                      |                                        (kiro-cli acp,
                      |                                         claude,
                      |                                         gemini,
                      |                                         codex, ...)
                      |
                  transport layer
                  (cloudflared today, telegram TODO)
```

- One browser WebSocket connection = one `racp` session = one freshly spawned
  agent subprocess = one ACP session.
- `racp` listens only on `127.0.0.1`. Public reachability is delegated to an
  existing Cloudflare Tunnel on your network.
- The terminal-style web UI is a single embedded HTML file (`src/ui.html`)
  compiled into the binary via `include_str!`.

## File layout

```
RACP/
├── Cargo.toml
├── src/
│   ├── main.rs     # config, ACP bridge, transports, single binary entry
│   └── ui.html     # embedded terminal-style web UI (served at /)
├── cloudflared/
│   └── config.yml  # example named-tunnel config (only for a fresh tunnel)
└── README.md
```

## Quick start

```sh
cd /Users/stefan/Development/RACP
cargo run --release
```

First launch, no config present: `racp` drops into an interactive setup and
writes `~/.racp/config.toml`. Re-run setup any time:

```sh
cargo run --release -- init
```

Then point a browser at `http://127.0.0.1:7842` to smoke-test locally, or at
your public hostname once the tunnel is wired.

## Configuration reference

`~/.racp/config.toml`:

```toml
# Transport picks how clients reach racp.
#   "cloudflared" — serves HTTP + WebSocket on `bind`, for an external tunnel.
#   "telegram"    — stub; long-polls a Telegram bot. Not yet implemented.
transport = "cloudflared"

# Local bind address. Keep it on loopback; public exposure is the tunnel's job.
bind = "127.0.0.1:7842"

# Command to launch the ACP agent. Either a bare name (resolved via $PATH) or
# an absolute path. For Kiro: `kiro-cli` with args ["acp"].
agent_cmd = "kiro-cli"
agent_args = ["acp"]

[telegram]
# Only used when transport = "telegram". Create with @BotFather.
token = ""
```

### Agent command examples

| Agent | `agent_cmd` | `agent_args` |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| Kiro CLI | `kiro-cli` | `["acp"]` |
| Claude Agent CLI | `claude` | check your install; may be `["acp"]` or similar |
| Gemini CLI | `gemini` | check your install |
| Codex | `codex` | check your install |

Confirm a working invocation by running it manually; a healthy ACP agent prints
nothing to stdout until it receives a JSON-RPC message on stdin.

## Expose via an existing Cloudflare Tunnel

If you already have `cloudflared` running (Proxmox LXC, Docker, systemd unit,
whatever), add one ingress rule to its config:

```yaml
ingress:
  # ... your existing rules above ...
  - hostname: racp.example.com
    service: http://<host-running-racp>:7842
  # keep the catch-all last
  - service: http_status:404
```

Route the hostname to the tunnel once from the machine that owns the tunnel
credentials:

```sh
cloudflared tunnel route dns <your-tunnel-name> racp.example.com
```

Reload `cloudflared`. That is it. WebSocket upgrades are forwarded by default,
so `/ws` needs no special flags.

## Put Cloudflare Access in front (strongly recommended)

Once a public hostname points at `racp`, anyone who finds the URL can drive
your local agent. Treat this as non-optional:

1. Cloudflare Zero Trust → **Access → Applications → Add application → Self-hosted**.
2. Application domain: `racp.example.com`.
3. Policy: allow only your email, passkey, or IdP identity.

Access injects a signed `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header on every request.
Validating it in `racp` is a TODO (see *Known gaps*).

## Wire protocol

### Browser ↔ racp (over the WebSocket at `/ws`)

All frames are JSON text.

**Browser → racp:**


**racp → browser:**


```sh

./target/release/racp           # run

```

stderr. For Kiro-side debugging:

```sh
KIRO_LOG_LEVEL=debug ./target/release/racp
```

(the env var is inherited by the spawned `kiro-cli acp` child).

All marked with `TODO:` in the source.

1. **Permission UI.** `session/request_permission` currently auto-allows the
   first offered option. The browser needs a button row and `racp` needs to
   forward options to it and wait for the response. See
   `handle_agent_message` in `src/main.rs`.
2. **Auth enforcement.** `racp` trusts everything that reaches the WebSocket
   upgrade. When fronted by Cloudflare Access, validate the
   `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header (JWKS at
   `https://<team>.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/certs`). See

3. **Telegram transport.** `run_telegram` is a stub. Planned shape: long-poll
   `getUpdates`, one ACP agent per Telegram chat, stream chunks as

   prompts. Per-user-token model (BotFather) keeps `racp` out of the data
   path.
4. **Kiro extensions (`_kiro.dev/*`).** Not forwarded. Useful candidates:
   slash-command availability (could become a UI affordance), MCP OAuth URL
   (needs user redirect), compaction status (status-bar hint).
5. **Session reuse.** Each browser reload kills its agent and spawns a fresh
   one. Reusing sessions via `session/load` would keep history across
   reconnects. Kiro persists sessions at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`.
6. **Streamable HTTP remote transport.** ACP's draft RFD defines an HTTP/WS
   remote transport with `Acp-Connection-Id` and `Acp-Session-Id` headers;
   today `racp` is purely a local stdio client. Once the RFD stabilises and
   agents support it, `racp` can become a thin remote adapter too.

## Development guide

### Build, check, lint

```sh
cargo check
cargo build --release
cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings   # team should gate on this
```

Rust edition 2021, MSRV follows the current stable toolchain.

### Where to add things

| Change | File and function |
|--------|-------------------|
| New `session/update` variant | `handle_agent_message` in `src/main.rs` |

- **Config round-trip.** Property test that `Config` survives

- `cargo run --release` prints bind address on stderr and forwards the

Handoff-ready. Quick summary of what the team will see when they open the repo:

- **`README.md`** (296 lines): overview, ASCII architecture, file layout, quick start, full config reference with agent command examples, Cloudflare Tunnel + Access setup, browser↔racp and racp↔agent wire protocols, numbered known gaps with source-file pointers, dev guide (where to add new transports / update variants / auth middleware), suggested test coverage, and a troubleshooting section keyed to real error messages.
- **`src/main.rs`**: module-level doc with architecture and wire shapes, section banners (config, entry, cloudflared, telegram, helpers, agent subprocess), rustdoc on `Agent`, `handle_ws`, `handle_agent_message`, `spawn_agent`, and `TODO(permission-ui|auth|telegram|kiro-extensions)` markers at each extension point the README references by number.
- **`src/ui.html`**: header comment inside the script block documenting the WS wire shape and the `connected → thinking... → responding → connected` state machine so UI changes have context.

Stefan wants me to update the .gitignore to exclude files that shouldn't be in the repo. For this Rust project on macOS, I need to add entries for build artifacts and platform-specific files, though I should keep Cargo.lock since this is a binary project that needs reproducible builds.

Beyond the standard Rust ignores, I'm thinking about macOS metadata files, editor configurations, environment secrets, and importantly the `.kiro/` directory since Kiro can create session state and agent configs in workspaces that shouldn't be committed. I'll keep the additions focused and minimal—no need to over-engineer the .gitignore.

Updating the file now... Finalizing the .gitignore patterns...

I'm debating whether to include the `.env.example` negation rule since the project doesn't currently use environment files, but it's a common convention that'll be useful if someone adds one later. For the vim swap files, `*.swp` should catch both regular and dotfile variants in git's pattern matching, so I don't need to add `.*.swp` separately. Keeping the cloudflared credentials pattern as defensive insurance even though they typically live in the home directory. I'll go with a clean, practical list that covers the essentials without over-engineering it.

Done. Intentional choices:

- **`Cargo.lock` is not ignored** because this is a binary crate; committing the lockfile pins reproducible builds across the team.
- **`cloudflared/config.yml` is kept**, only `.json` credentials under that dir are ignored. The YAML is a template with placeholders, safe to commit.
- **`.env.example` is whitelisted** if anyone adds one later.
- **`.kiro/` ignored** because Kiro drops session state and workspace-local agent configs in there, none of which belong in the repo.

data.text:
Stefan's pointing out the permission UI gap I noted in the README. Right now when the agent requests permission for a tool call, we just auto-approve the first option, but a proper implementation would show the user what tool is being called and its parameters before they decide.

I need to design the wire protocol for this: the browser would send a `permission_response` message back to racp with the user's choice, and racp would send a `permission_request` message to the browser containing the tool call details and available options like "Allow once", "Allow always", or "Deny".

On the Rust side, I'd need to track pending permission requests in the session state, forward the agent's request to the browser instead of auto-replying, then when the response comes back, send it to the agent. For the UI, I'd render a permission card with buttons in the terminal style, keeping the input disabled while waiting for the user's decision.

Stefan's asking for clarification on what the permission UI entails—I should explain that it's the interface where users approve or deny tool execution requests, then offer to build it since it's a fairly contained piece of work.

content.data:
Quick refresher on what that gap is.

**What ACP does**

When the agent wants to run a tool that the client marked as needing approval (by its autonomy policy), it sends a `session/request_permission` with:

- A `toolCall` summary: tool name, arguments, human-readable title.
- An `options` array: typically `[{optionId: "allow_once"}, {optionId: "allow_always"}, {optionId: "reject_once"}]`, agent-chosen.

It blocks waiting for your reply. Your reply picks one `optionId`, and the agent proceeds (or doesn't).

**What racp does today**

Auto-picks the first option and moves on. Fine for a sketch, wrong for anything you would actually run over Cloudflare Access. If the agent's first option ever happens to be a deny, we silently deny. If it is a dangerous allow, we silently approve.

**What the UI needs**

1. New browser-bound message when the agent asks for permission, carrying the `toolCall` summary and the option list.
2. A small inline card in the log with a button per option. Input stays usable for cancel / context, but the turn is effectively paused until a button is clicked.
3. New browser-to-racp message with the chosen `optionId` and the original request id.
4. Server-side: track the pending permission id → oneshot mapping so the reply from the browser can unblock the agent.

About 60 lines of Rust plus a handful of lines in `ui.html`. Small, self-contained, and it replaces the TODO marker we already left in `handle_agent_message`.

Want me to implement it now at `/Users/stefan/Development/RACP`?
You might as well do it, go!

data.text:
I'm working through the permission UI implementation, starting with the Rust side. The key insight is that I don't need server-side tracking of pending permissions—I can just forward the permission request ID to the browser and use the agent's respond method directly when the browser sends back its answer.

But there's a security concern: a malicious browser could send fake permission responses with arbitrary IDs to approve tools that were never requested. For a local single-user tool this is low risk, but I should track pending IDs for better hygiene. I'll use a HashSet or HashMap to store pending permission IDs, validate incoming responses against it, and only then forward the response to the agent. Since `serde_json::Value` doesn't implement Hash due to float equality issues, I'll convert IDs to strings for the key—that's simpler and always works.

Actually, wait. The browser already has full control over the agent through the prompt interface, so a fake permission response doesn't grant additional power beyond what a malicious prompt could do. The only real concern is if the browser is compromised or there's a cross-site attack, but since we serve the UI ourselves on the same origin with Cloudflare Access auth, the browser session IS the user. I'll keep it simple and skip server-side tracking—just forward IDs unchanged in both directions.

So the plan: remove the auto-allow logic from `handle_agent_message` for permission requests and forward them to the browser instead. Handle the new `permission_response` message type in the WebSocket loop and call `agent.respond()` with the ID and outcome. On the browser side, I'll render an inline card when a permission request arrives, let the user pick an option, send the response back with the ID and selected option, then remove the card.

Defining the message shapes...

Implementing main.rs changes...

I need to restructure the message handling to use a match statement on the type field instead of the rigid if-check. For `permission_response`, I'll extract the id and optionId, then spawn an async task that sends the response back to the agent with the selected option. Then I'll add the UI handling to render permission request cards and send responses when the user clicks an option.

Now I'm thinking through the interaction flow — the agent blocks while waiting for permission, so input is already disabled during that window since we're mid-turn. The user can still send a new prompt if they want, but it'll queue up. I'll keep it simple and not add extra blocking logic.

For the implementation, I'm switching the select loop to use pattern matching instead of a simple type check, so I can handle both prompt and permission_response messages cleanly. Then I'll update the UI to render the permission card with styled buttons, wire up the response sending, and make sure the backend processes those responses correctly.

Let me start with the Rust changes...

input.newStr:
1. **Auth enforcement.** `racp` trusts everything that reaches the WebSocket
   upgrade. When fronted by Cloudflare Access, validate the
   `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header (JWKS at
   `https://<team>.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/certs`). See
   `ws_upgrade` in `src/main.rs`.
2. **Telegram transport.** `run_telegram` is a stub. Planned shape: long-poll
   `getUpdates`, one ACP agent per Telegram chat, stream chunks as
   `editMessageText` throttled to ~1/s, inline keyboard for permission
   prompts. Per-user-token model (BotFather) keeps `racp` out of the data
   path.
3. **Kiro extensions (`_kiro.dev/*`).** Not forwarded. Useful candidates:
   slash-command availability (could become a UI affordance), MCP OAuth URL
   (needs user redirect), compaction status (status-bar hint).
4. **Session reuse.** Each browser reload kills its agent and spawns a fresh
   one. Reusing sessions via `session/load` would keep history across
   reconnects. Kiro persists sessions at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`.
5. **Streamable HTTP remote transport.** ACP's draft RFD defines an HTTP/WS
   remote transport with `Acp-Connection-Id` and `Acp-Session-Id` headers;
   today `racp` is purely a local stdio client. Once the RFD stabilises and
   agents support it, `racp` can become a thin remote adapter too.

content.data:
Done. Restart the binary (the UI is embedded via `include_str!`) to pick it up.

**Behaviour**

- When the agent sends `session/request_permission`, you get an inline card in the log with the tool title (orange left border to catch the eye) and one button per option the agent offered.
- Buttons are coloured: green tint for anything whose `kind`/`optionId` starts with `allow`, red tint for anything starting with `reject`.
- Click resolves the card: the card fades, buttons disappear, a `→ <choice>` line is appended so the history stays readable. Your selection is sent back to the agent, which unblocks.
- Input is already disabled during a prompt turn, so the flow is: send → agent asks → you click → agent finishes → input re-enables on `prompt_done`.

**Changes on disk**

- `src/main.rs`: `handle_agent_message` now forwards `session/request_permission` as a `permission_request` WS event; the select loop dispatches `prompt` / `permission_response` types and no longer ignores non-prompt messages.
- `src/ui.html`: CSS for `.perm` card + buttons, `renderPermission` JS, new `permission_request` case in the WS message switch.
- `README.md`: wire protocol now lists the two new message types, and the "Permission UI" entry is gone from known gaps (remaining gaps renumbered).

I wrote "Nice, thank you" after the agent's reply, but there is no new line? Can we make sure there is always one?

data.text:
Stefan's hitting a formatting issue where messages run together without proper spacing. The agent's response stream ends without a trailing newline, so when the user types their next message, it concatenates directly to the agent's last chunk instead of appearing on a fresh line.

I'm weighing a few approaches: add a newline when the agent finishes streaming, add one before the user appends their message, or do both defensively. The cleanest solution mirrors how terminals work—each message should start on its own line. I'll implement a helper that checks if the log ends with a newline before appending the user's message, ensuring the `> ` prefix always appears on a fresh line.

Writing the newline check logic... I'm refining the approach to check if the last child element is a span and whether it ends with a newline, adding one before the user prompt if needed to keep the formatting clean.

content.data: Good catch. The agent's last chunk often doesn't end with `\n`, so your `> ...` lands on the same line. Fix is in the UI: ensure a newline before appending the user prompt.

data.text:
Stefan wants multiple tabs for multiple sessions. I'm clarifying what this means—it could be multiple browser tabs each opening their own WebSocket connection to spawn separate agent instances, which actually already works since users can just open the URL in different tabs and each one creates its own session.

But the more useful interpretation is a tab bar within a single page, where each tab represents a separate session with its own WebSocket connection, log area, and input field. This lets users context-switch between sessions without juggling multiple browser windows. The server doesn't need any changes since it already spawns an agent per WebSocket connection—I'd just need to refactor the client-side JavaScript to manage multiple sessions, track which one is active, and swap the UI to show the right session's logs and controls. This is a moderate UI change, probably around 100-150 lines of JavaScript to implement the tab bar, session management, and DOM swapping.

Beyond the core multi-tab feature, there are several other quick wins I could add: keyboard shortcuts for common actions, automatic reconnection with backoff when the WebSocket drops, a cancel button to stop the current turn, and some mobile-friendly CSS tweaks. Markdown rendering would require pulling in a library which adds complexity, and session persistence via localStorage or server-side storage is doable but adds more scope. The real question is what Stefan considers "quick"—multi-tab is definitely achievable in a focused sprint, but I should clarify which of these extras would actually be useful versus nice-to-have.

He specifically mentioned multiple tabs as a suggestion, so that's the headline feature. The value proposition is solid: on mobile you can't easily juggle browser tabs, and in-page tabs let you quickly switch between conversations without losing context or dealing with OS-level tab management. I'll propose multi-tab as the main win and mention cancel + reconnect as natural companions since they're genuinely tiny to implement. I'll sketch out the basic structure—a header with tab controls and a pane container below—and see what he wants to prioritize.

Building the session architecture... I'm laying out the tab bar styling with flexbox, setting up individual tab elements with hover states and close buttons, then configuring the panes container to display one log at a time.

Now I need to restructure the HTML to use a tabs container and separate panes for each session, then refactor the JavaScript so each session manages its own WebSocket connection, log element, and state variables instead of relying on global ones.

Refactoring the JavaScript... Building the permission UI...

Now I'm setting up the message handler to parse incoming WebSocket data and route it based on message type—handling text appends, permission requests, completion signals, and errors. Then I'm establishing the WebSocket connection with appropriate status callbacks for open, close, and error states, plus a message handler. Finally, I'm starting to render the tab interface by clearing and rebuilding the tabs container.

Building the tab UI... I'm checking whether `crypto.randomUUID()` will work in the local environment—it should be fine since `http://127.0.0.1` is treated as a secure context. Now I'm going to rewrite the entire ui.html file from scratch since the changes are substantial enough that a fresh create is cleaner than trying to patch sections.

Writing the HTML structure... Still writing styles... Writing the client-side JavaScript... Still writing permission handler... Building the UI components... I'm realizing the keyboard shortcuts conflict with browser defaults—Cmd+T opens a new tab and Cmd+W closes it, so my handlers won't even fire. Rather than fight the browser, I'll drop the shortcuts entirely and rely on the UI button; the title attribute provides a hover hint anyway. I should also update the README to mention the multi-tab feature, and then write out the final file.

content.data:
Done. Restart the binary, reload the page.

**What you get**

- Tab bar in the header. Each tab is its own `/ws` connection = its own agent subprocess = its own ACP session.
- `+` button to spawn a new tab / session.
- `×` on a tab closes it, killing that agent (shown only when more than one tab exists, so the UI can't strand itself).
- Status indicator in the header now reflects the **active** tab's state. Background tabs keep running; their agent chunks still stream into their own log in the background.
- The single input at the bottom drives whichever tab is active. Busy state is per-tab, so a tab mid-prompt can coexist with another tab idle.
- Existing features preserved: thinking/responding status, permission cards, ensure-newline before `> ...`.

**What I deliberately skipped**

- Keyboard shortcuts. `Cmd+T` / `Cmd+W` conflict with the browser, and alternatives like `Alt+T` feel arbitrary. Easy to add later if you want them.
- Session labels. Tabs are numbered `1`, `2`, `3`. Auto-naming from the first user message would be nice but is speculative and small; leaving it for later.
- Persistence across reloads. A reload still means fresh agents. ACP has `session/load` and Kiro persists sessions at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`, so this is the hook if you want it.

**Other quick wins I could smash out next if you want**

1. **Cancel button** — hit `session/cancel` on the active turn. Tiny patch server-side (send a notification) and a button in the header. Useful when the agent goes off on a tangent.
2. **Reconnect with backoff** — on WS close, auto-retry the transport connection instead of leaving the tab dead. Survives tunnel blips.
3. **Scroll-pinning** — if the user scrolls up, stop auto-scrolling on new chunks; snap back when they scroll to the bottom. Tiny UX win for long agent replies.
4. **Labelled tabs** — double-click a tab to rename it, or auto-label from the first user message (truncated).
5. **Session restore** — persist `(sessionId, label)` in `localStorage` and try `session/load` on reconnect. Small Rust change too.

content.data:
All five shipped. Rebuild and reload.

**What you get**

- **Session labels.** Numbers by default; double-click any tab to rename. Prompt dialogue for now.
- **Persistence across reload.** localStorage holds `{id, label, acpSessionId, activeId, nextLabel}`. On reload, each tab reconnects with `/ws?session=<id>`; the server calls `session/load` and the agent replays history, which the UI clears-and-refills once the `ready` message arrives with `resumed: true`. If the load fails (stale id, agent restart), server falls back to `session/new` and the UI keeps its current log.
- **Cancel on active run.** `cancel` button appears in the header whenever the active tab is busy. Click sends `{type:"cancel"}` → server sends `session/cancel` notification → agent aborts the current turn and eventually resolves `session/prompt`, which re-enables the input.
- **Reconnect with backoff.** WS close while `closing: false` kicks off an exponential backoff retry (500ms, 1s, 2s, ..., capped at 30s). One `[disconnected, reconnecting...]` line per outage, no log spam. On successful reconnect, `session/load` rehydrates.
- **Scroll pinning.** Each log tracks `pinnedToBottom` via a scroll listener; new chunks only force-scroll when you're within 20px of the bottom. Scroll up to read backlog, scroll back down to re-pin.

**Server-side changes**

- `ws_upgrade` extracts `?session=<id>`; `handle_ws` uses it for `session/load` with a `session/new` fallback.
- New `ready` message carries `{sessionId, resumed}` after handshake.
- New `cancel` browser message → `Agent::notify("session/cancel", ...)`.
- New `Agent::notify` for one-way JSON-RPC calls.

**README updates**

- Wire protocol section lists the three new messages.
- "Session reuse" known gap removed; remaining gaps renumbered.

`cargo check` clean.

- **Run shell commands** and CLI tools on your machine (git, cargo, npm, terraform, docker, etc.).
- **Work with AWS** — query and manage resources via the AWS CLI, look up regional availability, search official AWS docs, CDK, CloudFormation, Amplify, Strands Agents.
- **Navigate codebases** with AST-aware search, symbol lookup, and structural refactors — not just grep.

- **Search the web** and fetch specific pages when I need something outside my training.
- **Coordinate subagents** in pipelines for bigger research or planning tasks, so the main thread stays focused on implementation.
- **Persist knowledge** across sessions — I maintain your knowledge base at `~/.kiro/knowledge` so we don't rediscover the same things repeatedly.

Stefan's pointing out that the agent knows its current working directory because I'm passing the launcher's `cwd` to the session — which works fine for a personal tool but could be something to revisit if this ever needs to handle multiple tenants.

The real issue is that on reload, user messages aren't showing up. Looking at my message handler, I'm only processing agent messages, agent thoughts, and tool calls, but I'm completely ignoring `user_message_chunk` notifications that come back during the session replay. The fix is straightforward — I need to add a handler for those chunks that appends them with the "user" role, just like the agent messages. I'm settling on prefixing user message chunks with `> ` since Kiro likely emits them as a single chunk rather than splitting them up. This keeps the replay format consistent with how live-typed messages appear, even if there's a slight visual difference between the two modes. during replay, but not during live sessions. So the real issue is distinguishing between the two: if I start handling `user_message_chunk`, live sessions might show duplicates (browser's local echo plus the chunk), while replay needs them to show user messages at all. I could either only process chunks during replay mode, or skip the local echo entirely and rely on chunks—but that's risky if Kiro doesn't emit them for live prompts. The safest approach is probably tracking whether we're still in replay, then ignoring chunks once we've finished loading history. Looking at the ACP spec more carefully, `user_message_chunk` appears in the resume/replay flow but not in the live prompt flow, which suggests it's specifically for loaded sessions. So the safest approach is to render `user_message_chunk` with the `> ` prefix and assume live user messages are already handled client-side — if duplication becomes a problem, I can address it then.

I'll add the handler for this and briefly acknowledge Stefan's observation about the working directory being tunable, without over-explaining since he was just noting it as useful context.

Now I'm thinking through the replay flow more carefully. If user_message_chunk arrives in multiple chunks, each one would get prefixed with `> `, which would be wrong. For typical typed messages it's usually a single chunk, so I'll proceed with that assumption. But I'm realizing the server-side needs to handle newline consistency too — if the agent's message doesn't end with a newline, the next user message starting with `> ` would concatenate awkwardly. Simpler approach: prepend a newline to each user_message_chunk on the server side, so the log naturally separates agent and user turns. I'm thinking through how to handle newline formatting for user messages in the replay. The key insight is that the server doesn't know the current state of the browser's log, so I need a format that works regardless — sending `\n> ${text}\n` ensures proper separation whether the prior content ended with a newline or not. This might create a small blank line between turns, but that's actually acceptable and mimics terminal behavior. For live messages, the browser already handles this smartly with `ensureNewline()`, so I just need the server-side replay to be similarly robust.

The cleanest approach: have the server emit `> ${text}\n` for user messages, and on the client side, call `ensureNewline()` before appending any user-role content. This way the browser handles the formatting logic consistently for both live and replay flows.

For live turns, the browser renders locally without waiting for the server echo. During replay, when user message chunks arrive from the server, the client-side `ensureNewline()` ensures proper spacing before rendering them. Since Kiro doesn't echo user messages during live turns, there's no risk of duplication in the current setup, though future agents might behave differently.

content.data:
Two quick answers.

**cwd**: yes, the agent sees `racp`'s own current working directory as the session's `cwd` (set in `handle_ws` via `std::env::current_dir()`). So if you launch `racp` from `~/Development/RACP`, the agent treats that as the project root. If you ever want per-tab cwd (different tabs scoped to different projects), it's a small extension: add `cwd` to the browser-side session state, pass it on the WS query or first message, use it in `session/new`.

**Missing user messages on reload**: real bug. Fix in one line on the server and one on the client.

Cause: ACP's `session/load` replays history via `session_update` notifications. It emits both `agent_message_chunk` (which we handle) and `user_message_chunk` (which we ignored). Per the ACP spec, `user_message_chunk` only appears during replay, never during live turns, so handling it can't cause a double-render on live prompts.

Stefan needs the ability to set a working directory when creating a new session, with the option to fall back to wherever `racp` was launched from if not specified. I'm thinking through how to store this per-tab in localStorage and pass it to the server during session creation or loading, so the server can use that directory or default to its own process working directory. There are a few design approaches to consider here.

Option A seems cleanest: pass the cwd as a URL query parameter like `?cwd=/Users/stefan/project` when upgrading to WebSocket, which the server can validate and use. On page reload, the cwd gets pulled from localStorage and included in the reconnect URL. For the UI, I'll use a simple `prompt()` dialog when clicking the "+" button to create a new session—if the user enters a path, it gets stored in the session state and URL-encoded in the WebSocket connection string; if they leave it blank, the server just uses its default.

For validation, since this is a personal tool behind Cloudflare Access, I can trust the authenticated user. The server should extract the cwd query param and use it if provided, otherwise fall back to the current working directory. If the path is invalid, the agent's file tools will fail anyway and Stefan will see the error. When resuming a saved session, I'll pass the original cwd from localStorage in the reconnect URL—the server uses it for both new and resumed sessions, so the cwd stays consistent across reloads.

On the client side, I need to add cwd to session state, prompt for it when creating a new session, include it in the connect URL, and persist it in localStorage. For the prompt UX, I'll keep it simple with no default value and a note that blank means the server uses its own directory.

content.data:
Done. Rebuild and reload.

**Behaviour**

- Click `+` → prompt appears: "Working directory for this session / (leave blank to use racp's directory)". Hit Enter for default, paste a path for scoped, or press Esc to abort.
- The cwd is persisted per tab. On reload / reconnect it rides along in `?cwd=<path>` and the server uses it for `session/new` or `session/load`.
- Hover a tab and the tooltip shows `"<label> · <cwd>"` when a cwd is set, so you can tell which tab is rooted where without expanding them.
- No server-side path validation. The authenticated user (via Cloudflare Access) is trusted with their own filesystem; if the path doesn't exist, the agent's file tools will error when they try to touch it.

**Changes**

- `main.rs`: `ws_upgrade` extracts `cwd` query param (trimmed, empty → absent); `handle_ws` takes it as `cwd_override: Option<String>` and uses it or falls back to `std::env::current_dir()`.
- `ui.html`: session state gains `cwd`; `connect` builds the URL with `URLSearchParams` so `session` and `cwd` compose cleanly; `+` now prompts; tab tooltip surfaces the cwd.
- `README.md`: mentions the new `?cwd=` query param.

How can I close a session tab?
Also, would be great to have a session history so I can restore a tab I closed in the past.

data.text:
I'm realizing there's a design issue with the close button — it only appears when there's more than one tab, which means if Stefan only has a single tab open, he can't close it at all. I need to reconsider this constraint and also think about how to handle session history so users can restore tabs they've closed.

For the close button, the better approach is to always show it. I already have logic that spawns a new session automatically if the sessions array becomes empty, so the UI will never be left blank. The `newSession()` call doesn't prompt for a working directory when triggered this way, so it's a clean experience.

For restoring closed tabs, instead of completely discarding a session when it's closed, I should preserve it in a history so users can bring it back later. The server-side ACP session persists to disk even after the WebSocket closes, so the session data is still recoverable.

I'll store closed sessions in localStorage with their ID, label, working directory, and timestamp, keeping the history bounded to the last 20 entries. The UI could be a dropdown menu, modal, or toggled panel that lets users see their closed sessions and click to restore one. When restoring, I create a new active session from the saved state and remove it from history.

For the implementation, I'm going with a dropdown menu in the header since it's the cleanest approach. I'll add the CSS styling to match the existing dark monospace aesthetic, update the state to track closed sessions, and wire up the restore and forget actions. When a session closes, if it has an active connection ID, I'll push it to the closed history and trim to the last 20. I should also handle the case where a restored session no longer exists on the server—the fallback will just create a fresh session with the same working directory. I'll add a simple time formatter to show relative timestamps like "just now" or "5 min ago".

Building the dropdown UI now... right let me implement:

Step 1: Always show close button (even for last tab).
Step 2: Close-history infrastructure.
Step 3: History dropdown UI.

Let me break into small commits.

Actually, I realize: for showing close × on the only tab, if user clicks it, newSession() should still be called to keep UI non-empty. But it prompts for cwd. That's actually kind of annoying — if you click × to close your only tab, you don't want to be prompted for a cwd. Hmm.

Options:
a) Don't prompt when replacing-only-tab; use empty cwd
b) Don't add the prompt complexity; just use plain "+" + separate "new with cwd" button

Hmm. Actually I think the prompt on "+" is fine even for auto-restore. Let me think again.

`closeSession` calls `newSession()` (no args) when sessions becomes empty. That doesn't prompt — only `newTabBtn.onclick` prompts. So closing the last tab just creates a new default session. Good.

OK let me implement.

Let me also think: should closed tabs be automatically restorable on page reload? Right now, "persistence" means currently-open tabs are restored. Closed tabs are NOT restored on reload — they should remain in history, accessible via UI.

So on load, sessions[] comes from saved.sessions (currently-open), and closed[] comes from saved.closed (history).

On reload, history should persist too. That's another point in saveState.

OK let me code this.

Starting with removing the guard that prevents closing the last tab, then I'll set up a closed array to track session history with id, label, acpSessionId, cwd, and closedAt timestamp. I need to update loadState and saveState to handle the closed array, and modify closeSession to push closed sessions there while keeping the history limited to 20 items. Then I'll add a history button and dropdown in the header to display and restore from closed sessions.

Let me start making these edits.

content.data:
Done.

**Closing tabs**

Every tab now has a visible `×`. Closing the last one auto-spawns a fresh default session, so the UI never goes empty.

**Session history**

- New `history` button in the header. Click it, dropdown appears with the 20 most recently closed sessions (newest first).
- Each row shows the tab label, its cwd if any, and a relative timestamp (`just now` / `5 min ago` / `3 h ago` / `2 d ago`).
- Click a row → session is restored as a tab. The WS reconnects with `?session=<id>`, server calls `session/load`, agent replays history. If the agent no longer knows the session id (Kiro pruned it, restart, whatever), the server falls back to `session/new` and you get a fresh tab with the same cwd.
- Small `×` on each row to forget that entry if you do not want to keep it around.
- The dropdown closes on outside click. It does not close on its own row clicks, so you can forget multiple entries in a row if you want.

History persists in the same localStorage blob (`racp.v1`) under a `closed` array, capped at 20. The cap is a constant (`HISTORY_MAX`) if your team wants to tune it.

New tab asks for working directory, can it also ask for session name (both optional).
Session name first, working dir below.

data.text:
Stefan wants the new tab flow to ask for session name first, then working directory, both optional. The current approach uses a single `prompt()` for the directory, but two sequential modals would feel clunky. I'm weighing between sticking with multiple `prompt()` calls for simplicity versus building a proper modal dialog with both fields for better UX.

The native `<dialog>` element is the way to go—it's cleaner than custom modals, handles modal behavior natively, and gives me a proper form with two input fields and action buttons. I'm building a dialog with session name and working directory inputs, both optional, plus Cancel and Create buttons. The form uses `method="dialog"` so button clicks automatically set the return value. When the dialog closes, I'll check if the user clicked Create, grab both input values, and pass them to `newSession()`, which now needs to handle an optional name parameter for custom session labels.

For keyboard behavior, I need Enter to trigger Create by default while keeping Cancel accessible via Escape. The tricky part is balancing DOM order (which determines the default submit button) with visual layout—I want Cancel on the left and Create on the right, but I also need Enter to submit Create. I'll handle this by ensuring Create is the default submit button, either through DOM ordering or by explicitly marking it, then use flexbox to arrange them visually as needed.

Setting up the dialog markup with a form...

Now I'm wiring up the JavaScript to handle the dialog interactions—getting references to the form elements, setting up the cancel button to close the dialog, and the form's submit handler to extract the input values, close the dialog, and call the newSession function. I also need to reset the inputs and show the dialog when the new tab button is clicked.

For the newSession function, I'm thinking through how to handle the label generation. If the user provides a custom name, I should use that directly. But the tricky part is managing nextLabel—should it only increment when we actually use it for an unnamed session, so that named sessions don't waste numbers from the counter?

Defining the session creation logic...

Styling the dialog...

Now I'm thinking through the implementation plan: add the dialog element to the HTML, wire up the new tab button to open it, handle the form submission to create a session with the provided name and working directory, and make sure the cancel button properly closes the dialog. I should also autofocus the name input and let the native Esc key behavior work. There's one thing to watch though—the current `newSession` function gets called when there are no sessions, so I need to handle that case where no name is provided.

Browser support for the dialog element should be fine since it's been widely available since 2022. Let me start writing the changes.

content.data:
Done. Rebuild and reload.

**Flow**

- `+` opens a modal dialog with two fields: *Session name* (top, autofocused) and *Working directory* (below, placeholder reminds you that blank = racp's dir). Enter submits, Esc or Cancel dismisses.
- Either or both blank → sensible defaults: blank name = next number, blank cwd = racp's own cwd.
- Numbered labels (`1`, `2`, `3`) only advance when the user doesn't supply a name, so naming a session doesn't "skip" the next unnamed tab's number.

Double-click on an existing tab still renames via the native prompt — left that alone since you didn't ask to change it, but I can upgrade it to the same dialog if you want consistency.
Is session history kept int the browser or in ~/.racp/history (or similar)?

data.text:
The session history lives in the browser's localStorage under the `racp.v1` key, which stores open sessions, the last 20 closed ones, the active session ID, and the next label—all scoped to the domain where racp is accessed. The actual conversation content itself is managed by the agent (Kiro) and stored separately in its own directory.

This creates a tradeoff: browser storage is simple and works great per-device, but it means different browsers, devices, and incognito sessions each have their own isolated history. For Stefan's workflow—accessing racp from both his phone and laptop via a tunnel—server-side history would actually make more sense since it would follow him across devices and survive browser data clears. Given that the conversation content is already server-side anyway and we're operating under a single-user model with Cloudflare Access, moving the history to the server seems like the right call here.

But first I should just answer his question directly: the history lives in browser localStorage, the session content is stored by Kiro, and I can explain the per-device implications. If he wants cross-device access, we can move it server-side.

content.data:
Browser, currently. Specifically:

- **History list** (labels, cwds, closedAt, sessionIds) lives in `localStorage` under key `racp.v1`, alongside the currently-open tabs. One blob per origin (so `localhost:7842` and `racp.example.com` keep separate histories).
- **Actual conversation content** is not in racp at all. Kiro persists every session on disk at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/<session-id>.{json,jsonl}`. racp just hands the sessionId back to the agent via `session/load` and Kiro replays.

Implications:

- Different browser / device / incognito window = different history list.
- Clear the site's data and the list goes with it, but the sessions themselves are still on disk; you just lose the labels, cwds, and close order.
- When you hit racp from your phone through the tunnel, you will not see the laptop's history, even though the sessions themselves are all still there on the machine running racp.

If you want the history to follow you across devices (phone and laptop seeing the same list), the natural move is to store it server-side in something like `~/.racp/history.json` and expose a small `/history` endpoint the browser reads and writes against. Say the word and I'll do it.
Yeah, history should be cross device. The actual conversation content is fine to be in Kiro, we don't want to deal with that.

data.text:
I'm thinking about moving the history and open tabs to a server-side store at `~/.racp/history.json`, with the browser communicating via HTTP endpoints. The key decision is what needs to be cross-device: the closed tabs history definitely, and the currently-open sessions would also be useful to sync across devices.

I'd keep `activeId` per-device since each device should track its own focused tab independently, but `nextLabel` should be cross-device to avoid label collisions. Since this is a personal tool with one user, last-write-wins concurrency is fine.

The API would be simple — just `GET /state` to fetch everything and `PUT /state` to save changes. I'd store the state in `~/.racp/state.json` with atomic writes using a temp file and rename. On startup, the client fetches the state to rehydrate, then debounces any changes back to the server. For race conditions between devices, I need to think through what happens when both the phone and laptop try to modify the same list simultaneously.

Now I'm planning the actual implementation. On the server side, I'll add endpoints for getting and putting state, reading and writing atomically to the JSON file. I don't need explicit locking — just write to a temp file and rename it, so the filesystem handles atomicity. On the client, I'll replace the localStorage save calls with a debounced sync function and fetch the state on startup.

The tricky part is making startup async. I'll wrap the initialization in an async function that fetches the saved state first, then restores the UI. For activeId, I'm going to keep it server-side too for consistency across devices — last write wins, which is simple and acceptable for a single-user tool.

The server state file will mirror what localStorage had: sessions array, closed sessions array, activeId, and nextLabel counter. The endpoints are straightforward — GET returns the JSON, PUT accepts the new state and writes it atomically.

On the client, I'll fetch the state on startup and set up a debounced PUT whenever anything changes. I can remove the localStorage logic or keep it as a fallback. For validation, I'll trust the client for now and just check the body size limit that axum already enforces.

For implementation, I need to add the `fs` feature to tokio for async file operations, then write the server routes to read and write the state file atomically using a temp file and rename pattern.

Writing the handler functions... I'm realizing I already have a `config_path()` function, so I'll use `state_path()` to keep them consistent. On the client side, I'm replacing the synchronous `saveState()` and `loadState()` with async versions that communicate with the `/state` endpoint—`fetchState()` to retrieve saved state on startup, and `doSync()` which debounces rapid changes so multiple updates batch into a single PUT request instead of hammering the server.

The startup flow becomes async now since we need to wait for the server response before initializing the rest of the app. I'm also dropping localStorage entirely since it adds no real resilience—if the server is down, the whole app is unreachable anyway since it depends on the WebSocket connection.

Now I need to plan out the implementation: add the server-side state path helper and route handlers, import the necessary axum types like `StatusCode` and `Json`, then update the client to remove all localStorage references and wire up the new fetch-based sync logic. For the axum router, I'll chain the GET and PUT handlers on the same `/state` route.

Setting up the imports...

content.data:
Moving it. Plan:

- `~/.racp/state.json` holds `{ sessions, closed, activeId, nextLabel }`, same shape as before.
- `GET /state` returns the JSON (or `{}` if none yet), `PUT /state` writes atomically via tmp + rename.
- Browser fetches on startup, debounced PUT on any change. localStorage is gone.

input.newStr:
The WS upgrade URL also accepts `?cwd=<absolute-path>` to scope the session
to a different working directory than `racp`'s own process cwd. When the
browser prompts on `+`, it passes the result here; absent or empty means
fall back to `racp`'s cwd.

### Cross-device UI state

The browser persists the open-tabs list, history, and numeric counter via
two endpoints:

- `GET /state` — returns the current state JSON, or `{}` if nothing stored.
- `PUT /state` — atomically replaces the state (writes to `.tmp` + rename).

Backing file: `~/.racp/state.json`. Any browser hitting this racp sees the
same tabs and history — useful when you move between laptop and phone
through the same tunnel. Actual conversation content stays with the agent
(Kiro at `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`); racp only stores labels, cwds, and
ACP session ids.

content.data:
Done. Restart and hard-reload.

**Behaviour**

- On startup the browser does `GET /state`. If the file doesn't exist yet (first run), you get a fresh session. Otherwise all tabs, their cwds, history list, active tab, and `nextLabel` come back.
- Every change (new tab, close, rename, cwd set, sessionId assigned from `ready`, activate) calls `scheduleSync()` which debounces `PUT /state` by 400ms. So a burst of updates during page load becomes one write.
- Writes are atomic: tmp file + `std::fs::rename`. Readers never see a partial file.
- localStorage is gone. If you open racp from your phone now, it sees the same tab list.

**Caveat**

- Last write wins across devices. If you have two browsers open at once and rename a tab in one while the other is still synced from before, the last one to PUT will overwrite. For a single user this is fine; if it ever becomes an issue we'd add optimistic concurrency with an `ETag`.

**Files**

- `~/.racp/state.json` (new): the state blob.
- `~/.racp/config.toml` (existing): unchanged.

Consider this case; I have several tabs open, all running commands, but I cannot look at all tabs at the same time. Can we add an icon (green dot, or something like it) to a tab when the agent has completed its response?

data.text:
Stefan needs a visual indicator—a green dot—on background tabs once their agent finishes responding, which should disappear when the tab becomes active. I'm tracking this with the existing state machine by adding logic to show the dot when `prompt_done` fires on an inactive tab, then clearing it on tab activation.

I could expand this to handle other states like permission requests (orange) and errors (red) with a priority system, but Stefan specifically asked for the completion indicator, so I'll start with just that. Actually, permission requests are worth including since they block the tab—I'll add both done and permission states with appropriate colors, keeping error as the highest priority if it comes up later.

Now I'm implementing the attention system with a small colored dot that appears on inactive tabs. I'll add an `attention` field to session state that tracks whether the tab needs attention, clear it when the user switches to that tab, and set it based on different events like when a response completes or a permission is requested. The dot renders to the right of the label with colors for each state—green for done, orange for permission, red for error—and I need to position it before the close button in the tab structure.

Writing the implementation code...

content.data: Good idea. Extending slightly: green for "done", orange for "permission pending", red for "error" — all of which are "this tab wants you to look". Clears when you activate the tab.

content.data:
Clean. Rebuild and reload.

**Behaviour**

- The cancel button is now a permanent part of the input row, on the left of the text field.
- Disabled by default. Enables the instant you send a prompt on the active tab. Disables again when that turn finishes (whether by completion, error, or your own cancel).
- Since the input row drives the active tab, the button always reflects the active tab's state. Switching tabs re-syncs all three controls (input, send, cancel).
- Per-tab busy state is still tracked independently: a background tab mid-turn stays mid-turn; activating it flips cancel back to enabled.

data.text:
Stefan wants to consolidate the connection status display—removing the reconnection messages from the session log entirely and instead showing all connection state updates in the header's top right corner, which already tracks per-tab status like connecting, connected, disconnected, and thinking states. I think option 1 is the way to go—the header status already shows 'disconnected' when the WebSocket drops, so the in-log message is just noise. I need to remove that `append()` call that writes `[disconnected, reconnecting...]` to the chat and strip out the `disconnectNoted` tracking. The header can handle communicating the connection state on its own, and I could even make it more informative by showing something like 'disconnected, reconnecting...' there instead of just 'disconnected'. Stefan wants a single global connection indicator in the header's top right, separate from the per-tab status. Since all tabs connect to the same server, I can track connection state globally by checking if any tab's WebSocket is disconnected, then compute and display that state.

Implementing connection state logic... Removing the log message entirely seems like the right call—Stefan just wants it out of the chat, and the header already shows disconnection status for the active tab anyway. Background tabs can reconnect silently without the user needing real-time updates. I'll strip out the append call and the disconnectNoted flag to clean this up.

content.data: Right, that line shouldn't be in the conversation log at all — the header status already goes to `disconnected` on drop. Just stop spamming the log, and make the header text a bit more informative.

content.data:
Done. Rebuild, reload.

**Behaviour**

- No more disconnect/reconnect noise in any tab's log.
- Header status text on the active tab reflects connection state: `reconnecting...` while the WS is down and the backoff is retrying, `connecting...` during the WS handshake, `connected` once the server's `ready` message lands.
- Background tabs reconnect silently. When you activate one that's still down, you'll see its state in the header.

I see. The "Connected" or "Connecting message" at the top right is per session, not global. This is confusing. Right next to it we have the "History" button which should be global. In this case, I think the connection status should be moved into the session, it just needs to be neatly integrated into it.

data.text:
Stefan's right — the connection status should live in each tab since every tab has its own WebSocket connection, not in the global header where it doesn't belong. I need to move the status indicator out of the header, remove that span, and add a small status display directly into each tab instead. Now I'm thinking about what form that per-tab indicator should take.

A small colored dot on the left of the label makes the most sense — it won't conflict with the attention indicator on the right, and it keeps the UI clean. I'll show it only when the connection isn't fully connected: no dot when everything's fine, an amber dot when connecting or reconnecting, and green when actively connected. But then I realize the tab also needs to show thinking/responding state, which is also per-session. Rather than cramming connection state, turn state, label, attention dot, and close button all into one tab, I could use a single unified status indicator that rolls everything together.

Actually, the attention dot already handles done/permission/error for background tabs, so I can keep that separate. For the connection state, I'll just show a dot when it's not connected — pulsing grey when connecting, amber when reconnecting, nothing when it's stable. For thinking/responding, Stefan wants those visible per-tab, so maybe a thin animated underline or glow while the turn is in flight would be minimal enough. Or I could just add status text after the label when it's not idle — like "1 · thinking" or "1 · reconnecting" — which makes tabs wider but is very clear and works well for a sketch.

I think the text approach is clearer than trying to cram in more visual indicators. So the tab would show the label, then a small status text if the state is non-default, then the attention dot and close button. I'll remove the status from the header and instead render it directly in each tab. The rendering rule is simple: if the status is "connected" (the default), don't show anything; otherwise show the status text. I need to remove the header status element, update setStatus to just change the stored status and trigger a tab re-render, and then add CSS to style the status text nicely in the tab.

Styling the tab status text... Refactoring the status update logic to call renderTabs() whenever status changes, which handles both active and background tabs with minimal overhead since status only transitions a few times per interaction cycle. I need to remove the direct DOM manipulation from activate() and let the full tab re-render handle syncing the status display.

For the attention dot and status text coexisting, I'll keep both since they serve different purposes — the status shows live state while the dot signals completed events that occurred in the background. Now updating the CSS to style the status text within each tab label...

Adjusting the styling...

Actually, I'm going to stick with text indicators for clarity rather than animated dots. The final approach is to remove the header status span, have setStatus update the state and re-render tabs, then display a small status text next to each tab label when it's not idle.

data.text:
I see the issue now—Stefan's getting `session/load failed` errors because he opened tabs without actually sending any messages. Kiro doesn't persist sessions to disk until something actually happens in them, so the sessionId returned from `session/new` might never have been materialized as a file. Looking at the Kiro documentation, sessions are stored in `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`, which means the session file probably doesn't exist until the first prompt is sent.

The real problem is that on the server side, the old unused sessionIds are still sitting in state.json. When Stefan reloads, all 12 tabs try to load those old sessionIds, they all fail, and each one falls back to creating a new session. The client updates with the new sessionId and saves it, so theoretically the next reload should be clean—but it's still generating noise on every reload cycle until things stabilize.

I could fix this a few ways: prevent persisting sessionIds until they're actually used (but that breaks resume capability), downgrade the log level to reduce noise, or have the client explicitly forget stale sessionIds when the server says `resumed: false`. The core issue is that ephemeral sessions never get persisted by Kiro, so their IDs remain in state.json as dead references.

Option E looks promising — only persist a sessionId to state.json after the user sends their first prompt on that tab. Until then, keep it in memory only. If they reload before using the tab, it gets a fresh session without triggering failed load attempts. I need to track which sessions have been "used" and only sync those to disk. I need to also persist the `used` flag in the state file so it survives page reloads—otherwise on reload, sessions lose their "used" status and might incorrectly get treated as fresh. The core fix is tracking whether a session has had at least one prompt, then only syncing and preserving the acpSessionId for those sessions, while unused sessions get dropped from history entirely. I'm working through the session persistence logic across different scenarios. The key insight is that `used` only matters for the initial save—once a session is persisted to disk, subsequent reloads just resume from there. I'm settling on setting `used = true` when a prompt is submitted, which handles the edge case where a reload happens before the sync debounce fires. Now I need to make sure scheduleSync triggers appropriately on prompt submit. Still writing client changes... Implementing the session tracking logic now. I need to set `used` based on whether `acpSessionId` exists, update the sync logic to null out the session ID for unused sessions, and only add closed sessions to history if they were actually used. I'll also make sure the form submission marks a session as used, and keep the server logging as-is since the fix will make it rare anyway.

content.data: Right, the fix is client-side. Don't persist a session's ACP id until it's actually been used (had at least one prompt). Fresh-but-unused tabs aren't on Kiro's disk, so resuming them is guaranteed to fail.

content.data:
Clean. Rebuild and reload.

**Behaviour**

- Open a tab, never prompt, reload → its entry in `state.json` has `acpSessionId: null`, so the WS connects without `?session=`, server calls `session/new`, no load failure logged.
- Send a prompt once → `used: true`, `scheduleSync()` persists the ACP id. From now on, reloads resume via `session/load`.
- Closing an unused tab no longer pollutes history either — only sessions that actually had a prompt make it into the recently-closed list.

Your existing `state.json` still carries stale ids from before this fix, so you'll see the load-failure lines on the next reload one last time. After that it's clean.
And because of that I see "starting agent..." in the session and it never gets ready or past that point.

data.text:
Stefan's reporting that after my change, restoring an unused tab gets stuck on "starting agent..." and never moves forward. Let me trace through what happens: when the page loads and restoreSession is called with a null acpSessionId, makeSessionState sets used to false, then attachLog creates the log pane with a "connecting..." status, but something's breaking in the connect() flow.

The client builds a WebSocket URL with URLSearchParams, and when both acpSessionId and cwd are null, it just connects to /ws with no query parameters. The server receives this in handle_ws, takes the else branch since there's no resume_session_id, and calls session/new. The "starting agent..." message gets sent from the server and displayed on the client, but then it seems to hang there — which means something in the initialize or session/new flow must be blocking.

I'm realizing there might be a DOM issue though. When the client receives a 'ready' message with resumed=true, it clears the entire log innerHTML, which would destroy the statusEl that I added as a child of logEl. Then when setStatus tries to update that orphaned statusEl, the text updates but it's no longer in the DOM, so it's invisible. But Stefan's case has resumed=false, so that path shouldn't even execute.

Let me reconsider what's actually happening with the stuck "starting agent..." message. The client sends the WebSocket message, server processes initialize and session/new as ACP requests, but if those responses don't arrive or the ready message never gets sent, the status would stay stuck. Stefan's 12 reloaded tabs each spawned agent processes that tried session/load, failed, and fell back to session/new — but maybe my fix is causing the fallback to not send ready, or the old acpSessionIds in state.json are interfering with the new session creation flow. I think I see it now — Stefan's seeing the "starting agent..." text stuck in the log because we switched from sending a visible "ready.\n" message to sending a JSON ready event that only updates the status banner. The text log never gets a new line to show completion, so it just stays frozen at "starting agent..." even though the connection is actually ready.

The cleanest fix would be to remove the "starting agent..." echo entirely and let the sticky status banner be the sole indicator of connection state, keeping the log purely for conversation. Or I could restore the "ready.\n" line so there's a clear visual endpoint in the log flow.

Actually, I think the best approach is to strip out both the startup chatter and rely on the status banner. If the session fails to initialize, we're already logging that to stderr and the client sees a reconnecting banner, which isn't ideal but is out of scope here. The main thing is removing that "starting agent..." noise so the log stays clean and the user isn't confused about being stuck.

Let me remove the `send_sys("starting agent...\n")` call from the server.

content.data:
You're seeing the log text "starting agent..." because that was a system line I emit at handshake start. Previously there was a matching "ready." line on success; when I moved status to the sticky banner I kept the startup line but the completion line is now implicit. Result: logs always end with "starting agent...", which reads like it's hung.

Fix: drop the startup line. Status banner at the top already conveys this; the log stays pure conversation.
